result:
The bell over the door of the shop rang in a polite, apologetic way, as if it would rather not be the first to speak. The place was long and narrow, furred with dust in the high corners and bright on the counters, as if someone had sketched it in with a careful hand and then smudged the edges. Shelves held things that did not belong on shelves: a thunderhead folded like a sweater, the smell of cut grass sealed in a jar, a pair of eyeglasses with rain caught in the lenses. A cracked teacup hummed. Between two bookends, the last ten minutes of an afternoon leaned, spine-out.

A woman stood behind the counter, neither old nor young in the way that mountains refuse to be told their age. Her blouse was the pale green of waiting rooms. She was writing in a ledger with a pencil sharpened to a polite point. The ledger looked as if it had been rebound too many times, its spine more scar than book. She looked up, and her face rearranged itself into welcome without making a fuss about it.

“What have you brought me?” she asked.

I had wrapped it in a scarf because I didn’t know what else to do. When I set it down, the scarf slumped away and there it was on the counter: my morning. It was the size of a soup bowl and made of something that wasn’t porcelain and wasn’t light, and inside it a thin grayness pooled like water in a ditch. If I tilted my head, I could see, faintly, the ghost of steam from a coffee mug, the suggestion of oranges in a colander, the fragile sleeve of silence before emails start arriving. But mostly, it was dull and a little sticky.

“It doesn’t start anymore,” I said. “I shake it, and it won’t start.”

She leaned over and peered into the bowl the way a watchmaker peers into a gear train. “How long has it been like this?”

“A while,” I said. “Weeks. Months? Honestly, I couldn’t tell you. It got heavier. Then it got quiet in a way that hurt. It’s like the light knows my name but not my face.”

She nodded. “Name?”

I gave it, and she wrote it down. In the ledger, the pencil left marks that looked like paths on a map. The page I could see had entries like Weighed a regret, two ounces and Mended a laugh, one loose stitch. The handwriting belonged to someone who did not hurry and did not apologize for it.

“Let’s see,” she said, and reached for my morning. She took it with both hands the way people take babies when they know what they’re doing. She set it on a velvet pad, pressed two fingers to the rim, and closed her eyes.

“You’ve been using it every day,” she said.

“I thought that was allowed.”

“It is. But you’ve been using it for everything. It’s doing work that isn’t its job. It wakes you, and then it holds your worry until lunch. It tries to keep your to-do lists from leaking into the sink. It walks the dog of your anxiety in a storm and comes back soaking. No wonder it won’t start.”

A tiny embarrassment flared somewhere under my ribs. “I tried all the usual things,” I said. “Different alarms. Stretching. Drinking water before coffee. Gratitude lists. Seeing friends. Avoiding news. Then gorging on news. I deleted social media on Sundays. I bought a clock with hands. I put my phone in another room like a time-out chair. I woke up earlier and then later. Nothing. It’s like the chime of the hour fell off the wall.”

She reached under the counter and brought out a dish of small gray pebbles. They were all labeled in a careful, square hand. She let me read them: Checking the weather every ten minutes. Apologizing for your own hunger. Carrying umbrellas you never open. Deciding the worst outcome early to save time. Saving the good candle. Telling yourself you are most yourself at 2 a.m. Remembering only the things you said wrong.

“We can take some weight off,” she said. “You pay with something you don’t need, or don’t need in that form. I take it, you get your morning breathing again. I can only take so much at once. People get faint if they stand up too fast.”

“People?” I asked, stupidly, and then saw, beyond her, a woman at a table with a paper bag breathing into it. She was coaxing a laugh back into existence, gentle as if she were helping a cat out from under a bed. A child in a yellow jacket was returning the sharp to a pencil labeled Wonder. A tall man with ink on his fingers watched a wall clock, which watched him back.

The woman behind the counter pointed at the dish. “First weight?”

“Checking the weather,” I said. “I don’t need to know the wind in four cities I won’t be in.”

She plucked the pebble and set it on the rim of the morning, where it melted like frost. The gray inside my bowl stirred, then made a small, startled sound. She nodded, satisfied. “Good. Again.”

“The candle,” I said. “I keep saving it.”

She smiled with professional approval. “A classic.” The pebble hissed and was gone. The gray thinned. I could see, beneath, something the color of eggshell coming clean.

“Deciding the worst early,” I said, and the pebble paused a fraction of a second on her fingertip as if reluctant to be moved. When it dissolved, the air above the bowl tasted briefly like rain on a tennis court. The ache under my ribs slackened.

She murmured, “Almost,” and then, “There is something else, but it’s not a pebble.”

I knew before I knew. In the same way you know a word is coming before it arrives, I knew what wanted to be named and was pretending not to. It was not fear of failure. That had a particular, lithe shape and we had met. This was duller and had more fingers.

“I’ll only write when I’m not tired,” I said. It sounded small, said in a shop in daylight; at home, at night, it was a law.

“Ah,” she said softly. “An amendment you stapled to your life. It thinks it is kindness.” She opened a drawer. Inside were tags, each with thread. She chose one and wrote on it with the same pencil: Only when not tired. She tied it to my morning with a neat bow and then snipped the thread and kept the tag. “I can’t dissolve this for you,” she said. “But I can keep it here a while. You’ll have to do the dissolving yourself, with the dreary magic of practice.”

“I hate dreary magic,” I said.

“Everyone does,” she said. “It’s why I have a shop. How does it feel now?”

I peered into the bowl. The gray was no longer covering but threaded through, like the shadow in marble. Underneath, there was warmth. The idea of eggs on toast existed with dignity. The possibility of reading three pages of a book without checking an inbox shivered into visibility. I could, I thought, stand by a window with my coffee and look at the tree not because the tree would teach me something, but because it was the tree and I was a person and we had eyes and mornings.

“It’s lighter,” I said. “It sounds… it sounds like the word ‘begin’ pushed up a hill.”

She pushed the bowl back toward me and rested the ledger’s weight on it for a second so it wouldn’t slide. “It will still be itself. There will be days you will resent it for not being spectacular. You may break your own heart with what you expect of an hour. That’s not your morning’s fault.”

“How much do I owe you?” I asked, and my hand went to my wallet although I suspected it was the wrong currency.

She waved the wallet away. “You’ve paid in kind. If you want, you can leave something for the general fund.”

“What’s the general fund?”

She pointed to a wide-mouthed jar on the counter. Inside were slips of paper folded in the tight, secretive ways of fortunes. The label on the jar said Extra Apologies, but below someone had added with a pen and a fair hand: And Rehearsed Disappointments.

I took a breath, and another. Then I reached into my chest the way you reach into a coat pocket you haven’t used in a while and found, linted and warm, I knew how this ends. I wrote it down on a scrap and folded it small and slid it into the jar. My shoulders rose. My morning, on the counter, woke with a sound like a kettle deciding instead to sing.

“Thank you,” I said.

She angled her head as if listening to water. “Try to put something in it that isn’t work right away,” she said. “An egg. A walk down the hall and back. Let it know its job is to open, not to carry.”

I wrapped the bowl back in the scarf. It pulsed faintly as if it were alive, which, perhaps, was only honesty.

At the door, the bell collected itself to ring and then, like a good friend, did not.

Outside, the afternoon had leaned closer to evening. The air tasted like a pencil on its first day of school. I stood there and put my morning carefully into tomorrow, and in doing so discovered that the pocket where it lived in me was not broken, only bruised.

On the way home, I took a street I didn’t know. A window held a plant pressed against the glass as if it wanted to see my face. A woman pushed a stroller and sang very softly without asking anyone’s permission. I passed a bakery where someone was yelling, not angrily, and flour hung in the air like a half-remembered joke. I did not check the weather, so the sky was permitted to surprise me by having a flock of pigeons threaded through it like a thought you lose on purpose so you can find it again.

Later, I would wake to the thin first light and think, Oh. This again. Thank goodness. And I would make the egg, and eat it leaning against the counter, and the tree would be a tree, and I would be a person, and there would be a small, honest beginning before everything else. That would be enough to start the rest.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
